title: Entretien Ben SAMARRIPAS
notes:
Corruption Risk Mapping

Interview Summary Report

 

Item

Details

Interview date

January 28, 2026

Interviewee

Ben SAMARRIPAS

Interviewee’s Position

Vice President & General Manager – LFB American Plasma

Interviewers

Sandra LASAGNI, Sandra LEZMI

1. Scope of responsibilities and activities

  • Function: Vice President & General Manager of LFB American Plasma, responsible for the daily management and strategic oversight of the U.S. plasma collection operations.
  • Scope and missions:
    • Oversees operations, quality, regulatory affairs, operational excellence, project management, and innovation.
    • Supervises finance and HR through dotted‑line relationships.
    • Manages interactions with LFB USA (Framingham) for shared services.
    • Leads U.S. and EU‑related plasma purchasing and sales activities:
      • Historically: third‑party plasma purchasing (U.S. blood banks, EU suppliers).
      • For the last 18 months: transition to selling plasma, mostly to Takeda and Biotest.
  • Operational processes covered:
    • Commercial: negotiation of plasma sales/purchases.
    • Procurement: kits, equipment, donor‑management systems, lab tests, cleaning, waste management.
    • Contractual review involving Legal, Quality, Regulatory, and Logistics.
    • Quality / Regulatory: responding to FDA, COLA, PPTA inspections.
    • Human Resources: involvement in recruitment processes for key center positions.

2. Third parties and interactions

2.1. Plasma buyers and sellers

  • Examples: plasma sales to Takeda or Biotest; purchase of non-therapeutic plasma from CSL or Kedrion via small brokers. Occasional incoming unsolicited purchase requests from unknown entities (e.g., Russia).
  • Nature of interactions:
    • Frequent contact by email, calls, virtual meetings.
    • Periodic business trips to Europe to negotiate contracts.
  • Volumes: Significant (20–30% of the time is dedicated to these transactions).
  • Risk: Corruption risk perceived as low due to collective oversight.
  • Controls mentioned:
    • Multi‑stakeholder reviews before finalizing contracts.
    • Legal and Quality systematically involved.
    • Only specific senior leaders have signature authority.
    • Clauses on anti‑corruption included in contracts.
    • For unknown supplier: systematically escalated and declined if necessary due to geopolitical risks.

2.2. Regulatory Agencies

  • Names and nature of interactions:
    • FDA: inspections, mostly unannounced.
    • COLA: clinical accreditation.
    • PPTA: scheduled inspections (notifications go to Regulatory), certification required for U.S. plasma sales.
  • Risks mentioned:
    • Extremely low to non‑existent: FDA strictly forbids receiving anything (not even lunch).
    • Ben has never seen corrupt behavior in 28 years in the industry.
  • Controls Mentioned:
    • Strict legal framework.
    • High level of independence from auditors.
    • Turning down any offer that could be perceived as influencing an audit.

2.3. Suppliers and service providers

  • Main services and providers:
    • Soft‑goods kits, donor‑management IT systems: Haemonetics.
    • Plasma testing: QualTex.
    • Biohazard waste: Stericycle (only one viable supplier).
    • Janitorial companies: local or national contractors selected through RFPs process with 2–3 bids.
    • IT services (handled globally, no local decision‑making).
    • Building rental.
  • Risks mentioned: Corruption risk could theoretically occur at center‑manager level however, managers cannot decide alone due to mandatory procurement process.
  • Controls mentioned:
    • Purchasing and Finance review all bids.
    • Standardized checklists used for comparable services.
    • Approval thresholds by role.
    • Legal reviews every contract.
    • Contracts signed by CEO or VP Finance & IT.

2.4. Recruitment

  • Nature of interactions: Multi‑level HR process: internal recruiter screening (LinkedIn / Indeed) → hiring manager → layered interviews → HR & Director of Operations → GM when relevant.
  • Risks mentioned: Theoretical possibility of conflict of interest (family members) but in practice, no known cases.
  • Controls mentioned:
    • Policy not to hire relatives in the same site.
    • Multi‑interviewer panels.
    • Structured screening by recruiter.

2.5. Gifts and hospitality / grants, donations or sponsorship / events

  • Nature of interactions:
    • No participation in sponsorships, donations, or events (e.g., no sports events invites).
    • Vendors occasionally propose dinners.
  • Risks mentioned: Low likelihood of undue influence.
  • Controls mentioned:
    • No acceptance of gifts and payment of own meals.
    • Prior consultation with top management for any doubt.

2.6. Professional associations

  • PPTA membership: Only for accreditation purpose (approx. USD 9,000/year).
  • No lobbying activities relevant to LFB (due to its small U.S. footprint).
  • No risk identified
  • No interaction beyond auditing and certification.

2.7. Potential acquisition projects

  • No current acquisitions - GM would be involved.
  • Historically deals only with well‑known plasma‑center operators (e.g., ImmunoTek).

3. Overall perception of corruption risk

  • Corruption risk is perceived as low due to:
    • Limited exposure to high‑risk environments.
    • Structured and collective processes.
    • High level of regulatory scrutiny.
  • Even if the company grows, risk will remain controlled because:
    • The same multi‑layer validation processes would still apply.
    • No individual can act alone.
